recipes.name: Nourishing Vasanta Vata Kichadi
recipes.description: This Kichadi is specifically designed for Vata Prakriti during the Vasanta (Spring) season. It is warm, moist, grounding, and easy to digest, which are essential qualities for pacifying Vata. The combination of rice, moong dal, sweet potato, and greens provides wholesome nourishment without being heavy, aligning with the season's call for lighter, yet satisfying, meals. Warming spices like ginger, cumin, and asafoetida support digestion and reduce Vata's tendency for dryness and gas. Fenugreek leaves also aid in digestion and add a balancing bitter note.

ingredients.name: Raw Rice (Basmati or Sona Masoori preferred)
Fresh Ginger
ingredients.quantity: 1 inch piece, grated or finely minced

steps.instruction: Rinse the raw rice and moong dal thoroughly under cold water until the water runs clear. For better digestion, you may soak the moong dal for 15-30 minutes, then drain.
In a heavy-bottomed pot or pressure cooker, heat the ghee over medium heat.
Add cumin seeds and asafoetida. Sauté until the cumin seeds splutter and become fragrant (about 30 seconds).
Add the grated ginger and sauté for another minute until its raw aroma disappears.
Add the diced sweet potato and sauté for 2-3 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Stir in the turmeric powder and coriander powder. Cook for 15-20 seconds, being careful not to burn the spices.
Add the rinsed rice and moong dal to the pot. Stir well to coat with the spices and ghee.
Pour in 4 cups of water and add salt to taste. Bring the mixture to a rolling boil.

If using a regular pot, reduce the heat to low, cover, and simmer for 20-25 minutes, or until the rice and dal are tender and the water is absorbed. If using a pressure cooker, close the lid and cook for 3-4 whistles on medium heat, then let the pressure release naturally.

Once the Kichadi is cooked, stir in the chopped fresh spinach and fenugreek leaves. Cook for an additional 2-3 minutes, stirring until the greens wilt. If using dried fenugreek leaves (Kasuri Methi), crush them lightly between your palms before adding.
Check the consistency; if it's too thick, add a little more hot water and stir. The Kichadi should be soft, moist, and easily digestible. Serve warm, optionally with an extra drizzle of ghee.
Enjoy this nourishing and grounding meal.

recipes.name: Spring Vata Balancing Root Vegetable & Red Lentil Kichari
recipes.description: This recipe is specifically designed to pacify Vata dosha, offering grounding, warm, and moist qualities essential for Vata individuals, especially during the changeable Vasanta (Spring) season. It incorporates sweet and warming root vegetables with easily digestible red lentils and basmati rice, enhanced with Vata-pacifying and subtly Kapha-balancing spices. Please note: While 'Beef' was listed as a primary ingredient, the 'Diet: Vegetarian' input was prioritized, hence this recipe is vegetarian.

ingredients.name: Red Lentils (Masoor Dal, split and husked)

steps.instruction: Heat ghee in a large pot or Dutch oven over medium heat.
Add mustard seeds. Once they pop, add cumin seeds and hing. Sauté for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add grated ginger and sauté for another minute.
Stir in turmeric powder, coriander powder, and cumin powder. Cook for 30 seconds, stirring constantly to prevent burning.
Add the rinsed red lentils, basmati rice, diced sweet potato, and carrots to the pot. Stir well to coat with the spices.

Pour in 6 cups of water and add rock salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer for 20-25 minutes, or until the lentils and rice are tender and the vegetables are cooked through. Stir occasionally to prevent sticking.
If the mixture becomes too thick, add a little more hot water.
Stir in the chopped spinach and cook for another 2-3 minutes until wilted.
Remove from heat, let it rest for a few minutes.
Garnish with fresh coriander leaves before serving.

recipes.viruddha_reason: The request for 'Primary Ingredients: Beef' directly contradicts the stated 'Diet: Vegetarian.' An Ayurvedic Chef cannot recommend beef for a vegetarian diet. This recipe has been crafted as a vegetarian option suitable for a Vata individual in Vasanta, prioritizing the dietary restriction.

recipes.name: Vata-Balancing Creamy Spring Porridge
recipes.description: This nourishing and grounding porridge combines the comfort of Basmati rice and oats with warming vegetables and spices, perfectly designed to pacify Vata dosha. The use of ghee, coconut oil, and warm milk provides deep moisture and sustenance, while the gentle warmth from black pepper and turmeric helps balance the Kapha tendencies of Vasanta (Spring), ensuring easy digestion and revitalization.

steps.instruction: Rinse Basmati rice thoroughly under cold water until the water runs clear. Set aside. If using steel-cut oats, you may soak them for 15-30 minutes, though for rolled oats this is not strictly necessary.
In a medium pot or Dutch oven, heat Ghee and Coconut Oil over medium heat.
Add finely chopped Onion and minced Garlic to the pot. Sauté until the onion is translucent and fragrant, about 3-4 minutes.
Add the diced Potato and diced Tomato to the pot. Sauté for another 5-7 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the vegetables begin to soften slightly.
Stir in the Turmeric and freshly ground Black Pepper. Cook for 1 minute more, stirring constantly, until the spices are fragrant.
Add the rinsed Basmati rice and oats to the pot. Stir to coat them with the spices and oils, lightly toasting for 1-2 minutes.

Pour in 2.5-3 cups of water (adjust based on desired consistency) and add salt to taste. Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce the heat to low, cover the pot, and simmer for 15-20 minutes, or until the rice and oats are fully cooked and most of the water has been absorbed. Stir occasionally to prevent sticking.
Once cooked, stir in the warmed Milk until well combined and the porridge reaches a creamy consistency. Heat gently for a couple of minutes without boiling rapidly.
Serve immediately. A tiny drizzle of extra ghee can be added just before serving for added richness and Vata pacification.

recipes.viruddha_reason: While some combinations like milk with onion/garlic or tomato can be considered mildly incompatible in their raw forms, this recipe mitigates potential issues through thorough cooking and the inclusion of warming spices (turmeric, black pepper), making it a harmonious and easily digestible dish for Vata when prepared as described. The ingredients are not combined in a way that creates strong antagonistic effects.

recipes.name: Vata-Pacifying Golden Root Rice
recipes.description: A warm, grounding, and nourishing one-pot meal specifically crafted to soothe Vata dosha, promoting stability and comfort. The sweet, moist, and well-spiced nature of this dish, enriched with digestive spices and the unctuousness of ghee, makes it ideal for balancing Vata, especially during the transitioning Vasanta (Spring) season. Eggs have been excluded to adhere to the vegetarian diet.

steps.instruction: Wash the Basmati rice thoroughly under cold water until the water runs clear. Soak it in 2 cups of fresh water for 20-30 minutes, then drain completely.
Prepare the vegetables: dice the carrot and potato into small, uniform pieces (approximately 1/2 inch cubes). Mince the garlic and grate the fresh ginger.
In a heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven, heat the ghee over medium heat. Once warm, add the mustard seeds and cumin seeds. Allow them to splutter for a few seconds until fragrant.
Add the minced garlic, grated ginger, and cinnamon stick to the pot. Sauté for about 30 seconds until aromatic, being careful not to burn the garlic.
Stir in the turmeric powder and coriander powder. Cook for another 10-15 seconds, stirring constantly, to bloom the spices.
Add the diced carrots and potatoes to the pot. Sauté for 3-5 minutes, stirring occasionally, to lightly cook the vegetables and coat them with the spices.
Add the drained Basmati rice to the pot and gently stir for 1 minute, ensuring it's well-coated with the ghee and spices.

Pour in 2.5 cups of water and add salt to taste. Bring the mixture to a boil, then immediately reduce the heat to low, cover the pot tightly, and let it simmer for 15-18 minutes, or until all the water is absorbed and the rice is tender.
Once cooked, remove the pot from heat and let it rest, covered, for 5-10 minutes. This allows the steam to redistribute and the grains to fluff up beautifully.
Gently fluff the rice with a fork. Remove the cinnamon stick. Garnish with fresh cilantro, if using, and serve warm immediately.

eye_captures.dosha_signal: Eyes are clear. Liver and Pitta are balanced.

individualFoods.name: Dosa

doshaReason.vata: Its sour and light nature from fermentation can increase Vata's mobile and subtle qualities, despite being warm.
doshaReason.pitta: The heating potency (Ushna Virya) and sour post-digestive effect (Amla Vipaka) strongly aggravate Pitta.
doshaReason.kapha: Its light, heating, and sour properties help to liquefy and expel Kapha.

individualFoods.ojasReason: The Ojas quality of Dosa heavily depends on the freshness of the batter and proper preparation, generally considered neutral if optimal.
individualFoods.ayurvedicNote: Dosa, a fermented dish of rice and urad dal, possesses heating potency and a sour post-digestive effect, primarily due to the fermentation process which also adds lightness.

ojasImpact.reason: The Ojas impact is heavily dependent on the freshness of the Dosa batter and preparation method; without these details, it is considered neutral for your high Ojas.

analysisResult.overallNote: For your Vata-Pitta Prakriti with dominant Vata, and especially with Psoriasis, Dosa is generally not recommended. Its heating and sour qualities will significantly aggravate both Pitta (potentially worsening Psoriasis) and Vata (increasing dryness and instability). In Vasanta Ritu, which requires lightness and detoxification, Dosa's fermented nature can hinder these processes and potentially increase Ama.
analysisResult.bestTimeToEat: If consumed at all, the best time would be during Pitta Kala (10 AM - 2 PM), when your digestive fire (Agni) is strongest, to mitigate its heavy and sour aspects.

modifications.tip: Ensure the batter is very fresh, ideally less than 12 hours old, to minimize sourness and Ama formation.
modifications.reason: Old or overly fermented batter increases sourness, heat, and Ama (toxins), which is detrimental for Vata, Pitta, and Psoriasis.

modifications.tip: Prepare Dosa soft and light (like Set Dosa), avoiding crispy, oily roast varieties.
modifications.reason: Excess oil and crispiness exacerbate Pitta and Vata, making the dish heavier and more aggravating for your conditions.

modifications.tip: Serve with a fresh, cooling coconut chutney rather than a very sour Sambar or highly pungent chutneys.
modifications.reason: Cooling and mildly spiced accompaniments help balance Dosa's heating and sour qualities, soothing Pitta.

modifications.tip: Incorporate balancing spices like a pinch of cumin, coriander, or fennel into the batter or chutney.
modifications.reason: These spices aid digestion, reduce gas (Vata), and help cool excess heat (Pitta) without adding aggravation.

modifications.tip: Keep portion sizes small and consume only when truly hungry, ensuring full digestion.
modifications.reason: Small portions prevent overtaxing Agni, which is crucial for Vata-Pitta individuals and for managing Psoriasis.

analysisResult.seasonalNote: In Vasanta Ritu, when the body needs to shed Kapha and prevent Ama accumulation, the sour and fermenting qualities of Dosa are generally counterproductive, potentially increasing Pitta and contributing to Ama, making it less suitable.

individualFoods.name: Dosa

doshaReason.vata: Fermentation and sourness can aggravate Vata, especially if batter is old or digestion is weak.
doshaReason.pitta: Its Ushna (heating) Virya, Amla (sour) Vipaka, and fermented nature significantly increase Pitta.
doshaReason.kapha: Heavy (Guru) quality combined with potential for Ama formation can aggravate Kapha, particularly in Vasanta.

individualFoods.ojasReason: The fermented, sour, and heating nature of Dosa can contribute to Ama (toxin) formation, which directly diminishes Ojas, especially detrimental for Psoriasis.
individualFoods.ayurvedicNote: Dosa, being a fermented preparation of rice and urad dal, typically possesses Ushna Virya and Amla Vipaka. While traditionally nourishing, its fermented nature can lead to an increase in Ama and an aggravation of Pitta and Vata, especially if the batter is old or excessively sour. For conditions like Psoriasis, such qualities are considered highly unfavorable.

ojasImpact.reason: For a Vata-Pitta individual with Psoriasis, the fermented, sour, and heating qualities of Dosa can create Ama and imbalance Agni, directly leading to a reduction in Ojas.

analysisResult.overallNote: For your Vata-Pitta Prakriti and existing Psoriasis, Dosa is generally not recommended. Its fermented, sour, and heating qualities can significantly aggravate both Pitta and Vata, and contribute to Ama formation, which is detrimental for Psoriasis. In Vasanta Ritu, which calls for lightness and detoxification, Dosa's heavy and potentially Kapha-aggravating nature is also unfavorable. It would be best to choose lighter, less fermented options.
analysisResult.bestTimeToEat: If consumed, only during lunch (10 AM - 2 PM) when Agni (digestive fire) is strongest, to aid in processing the heavy and fermented qualities.

modifications.tip: Ensure the batter is very fresh, fermented minimally (6-8 hours at most), and not overly sour.
modifications.reason: Minimizes the Amla (sour) Rasa and Ushna (heating) Virya, reducing Pitta and Ama formation.

modifications.tip: Prepare dosas that are soft and light, not very crispy or oily ('roast' style).
modifications.reason: Reduces the Guru (heavy) and Snigdha (oily) Gunas, making it easier to digest and less Kapha-aggravating.

modifications.tip: Add a pinch of Hing (asafoetida) and ginger to the batter, and serve with a cooling, non-fermented coconut chutney.
modifications.reason: Hing and ginger aid digestion and reduce Vata aggravation from fermentation. Cooling chutney helps balance Pitta.

modifications.tip: Consider alternative batters made from lighter grains like moong dal (green gram) for a less aggravating option.
modifications.reason: Moong dal is tridoshic and lighter to digest, reducing the potential for Ama and dosha aggravation.
Reduces the overall load on Agni and mitigates dosha aggravation.

analysisResult.seasonalNote: In Vasanta Ritu, Dosa's heavy, fermented, and Kapha-aggravating qualities are counterproductive to the season's requirement for light, drying, and purifying foods to help eliminate accumulated Kapha.

modifications.reason: Freshly made and softer dosa is easier to digest and less likely to aggravate Vata and Pitta than crispy, old-batter restaurant versions.

modifications.tip: Request minimal oil or cook with Ghee at home.
modifications.reason: Excessive oil, especially low-quality restaurant oil, increases heaviness and Ama, further aggravating Pitta and Vata.

modifications.tip: Pair with mild, cooling coconut chutney instead of very spicy sambar.
modifications.reason: Coconut chutney helps balance the heating effect of the Dosa and can soothe Pitta.
The heating and sour qualities can exacerbate skin conditions like Psoriasis.

analysisResult.seasonalNote: During Vasanta (Spring), it is generally advised to consume lighter, drying foods to balance Kapha. Heavy, sour, and fermented restaurant Dosa can increase Ama and hinder the body's natural cleansing processes during this season, especially for a Vata-Pitta individual with Psoriasis.

foodItems.name: Dosa
foodItems.ojasReason: Restaurant-prepared dosa, especially if crispy and oily (roast style), is difficult to digest and lacks the fresh vitality (Prana) needed to build Ojas.
foodItems.recommendationReason: For your Vata-Pitta Prakriti with dominant Vata, and especially with Psoriasis, this restaurant Dosa is not advisable. Its heating, sour, and fermented nature, amplified by potentially excessive oil and crispiness from restaurant preparation, will strongly aggravate both your Vata and Pitta. This can directly trigger or worsen your Psoriasis by increasing inflammation (Pitta) and contributing to Ama (toxin) formation, which is particularly undesirable during Vasanta (Spring) when your body is naturally trying to cleanse.
foodItems.modifications: The restaurant-style crispy and oily preparation, combined with fermentation, can increase Vata.
foodItems.ayurvedicNote: Dosa, typically made from fermented rice and lentil batter, carries heating and sour qualities. While a soft, freshly made dosa can be nourishing, restaurant versions often become Pitta-aggravating due to excessive oil and fermentation, potentially increasing Ama (toxins).

foodResults.name: Dosa
foodResults.question_asked: Is the batter fresh (less than 2 days old)?

foodResults.name: Mutton Biryani
foodResults.classification: Rice with Mutton — Heavy Rajasic Feast Food
foodResults.dosha_summary: Kapha-Pitta aggravating; beneficial for Vata when prepared correctly with digestive spices
foodResults.vipaka: Guru (Heavy — Madhura with heavy post-digestion load)
foodResults.agni_impact: Requires maximum Agni (Tikshna Agni) to digest without Ama. The whole spices are Deepana but the heavy fat and protein dominate post-digestively.
foodResults.best_meal_time: Lunch only — earliest possible (11:30 AM–1 PM). Never at dinner. Never on empty stomach.
foodResults.question_asked: Is there a visible orange oil/fat layer floating on top of the biryani, and does the meat pull apart easily or remain tough?
foodResults.positive_label: Moderate Glistening, Tender Meat that Falls Off Bone
foodResults.negative_label: Visible Fat Layer Pooling, Tough Chewy Meat
foodResults.answer_given: Moderate Glistening, Tender Meat that Falls Off Bone
foodResults.reasoning: Tender meat confirms proper slow cooking (Dum method) which breaks down collagen into gelatin — making heavy protein digestible. Moderate glistening (not pooling) means appropriate fat used. Well-cooked biryani has harmonized flavors — rice, spice, and meat are one.
ideal_seasons: Hemanta (Nov–Dec) — Agni is at maximum strength
avoid_seasons: Sharad (Sep–Oct) — Pitta is elevated; adding Ushna-Guru mutton creates inflammatory cascade
foodResults.ritucharya_reason: Mutton is the heaviest meat in Ayurvedic classification. Only the body's winter-maximum Agni can process it without accumulating Ama in the Mamsa and Meda dhatus.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_vata: Best meat for Vata — heavy, warm, nourishing, and specifically Brimhana for Vata's depleted Dhatus. With proper digestive spices, this is Vata's feast food.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_pitta: Limit to once a month maximum. Ushna virya of mutton directly aggravates Pitta. Never eat with hot chutneys.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_kapha: Minimize or avoid — the heaviest food for the heaviest dosha. If eating, small portion with maximum pepper and no dessert after.
pairings_ideal: Plain Raita — probiotics for heavy protein
Fresh lime with salt — digestive Agni support
pairings_avoid: Paal Kolukattai (17) — Meat + Milk: critical Viruddha Ahara (skin disorders, allergic cascade)
Any cold drinks — suppresses Agni needed to process meat
Medu Vadai (12) or any other fried food in same meal

condition_warnings.advice: High purine content. Contraindicated during flare-ups. Strict limitation even in remission.
Avoid restaurant versions. Home version with good quality meat and ghee, once a month, may be acceptable under Vaidya guidance.
Contraindicated — Ushna virya of mutton + Pitta disorders = inflammatory aggravation.

red_flags: Visible orange-red oil pool at bottom of container
Tough, chewy mutton pieces (old meat or under-cooked)
Very heavy, bloated feeling lasting 4+ hours
Unnaturally yellow or orange colored rice

foodResults.name: Sambar Satham
foodResults.classification: Spiced Lentil-Vegetable Rice — Comfort Meal with Therapeutic Depth
foodResults.dosha_summary: Pitta-Vata pacifying; Kapha should moderate portion
foodResults.virya: Sama (Balanced — the Ushna spices of Sambar neutralize the Sheeta of rice)
guna: Guru (Slightly Heavy — rice + dal combination is more filling than individual components)
Mridu (Soft — mushy texture is the correct preparation)
foodResults.agni_impact: Deepana-Brimhana — the Sambar spices (mustard, cumin, pepper, turmeric, tamarind) kindle Agni while the rice-dal matrix provides Brimhana (nourishment). The one-pot cooking ensures spice oils permeate every grain — a superior absorption versus eating rice and Sambar separately.
foodResults.ama_risk: low when mushy; moderate if grains are separate and undercooked
foodResults.best_meal_time: Lunch — the heavier Guru guna needs strong midday Agni for full digestion. Never as dinner.
foodResults.question_asked: Is the Sambar Satham a sticky, mushy, aromatic preparation, or does it look like dry rice floating in watery Sambar with no integration?
foodResults.negative_label: Dry Separate Grains in Watery/Oily Sambar — Assembly Style
foodResults.answer_given: Dry Separate Grains in Watery/Oily Sambar — Assembly Style
foodResults.reasoning: Separate, firm grains in thin Sambar is a hotel shortcut — yesterday's rice reheated with today's Sambar poured over. The grains are retrograded, the Sambar has lost its Deepana volatile oils overnight, and the combination creates Guru-Ama load. A very oily surface indicates cheap fats (Dalda/refined oil) used in the Sambar tadka instead of ghee.
ideal_seasons: Hemanta (Early Winter: Nov–Dec) — warming spices offset cold
Shishira (Winter: Jan–Feb) — Brimhana quality suits the season's need for nourishment
Varsha (Monsoon: Jul–Aug) — Deepana spices support weakened Agni
avoid_seasons: Grishma (Summer: May–Jun) — reduce portion, add more tamarind/water to lighten
Sharad (Autumn: Sep–Oct) — Pitta season; reduce pepper and mustard quantity
foodResults.ritucharya_reason: Sambar Satham is a one-pot meal with inherent thermal balance — the warming Sambar spices prevent the rice's Sheeta virya from suppressing Agni. In summer, it is best eaten lightly portioned and with extra Sambar liquid to maintain hydration and lightness.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_vata: Excellent — warm, soft, moist, one-pot meal is ideal for Vata. Add a generous teaspoon of ghee on top before eating. Eat freshly cooked, never refrigerated leftovers.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_pitta: Very good — reduce pepper in Sambar component slightly. The dal protein and cooling vegetables pacify Pitta. Avoid adding extra pickle or sour sides.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_kapha: Acceptable once weekly — the grain-dal combination is too Guru for daily Kapha use. Reduce rice ratio, increase vegetable content, avoid ghee topping.
pairings_ideal: Appalam/Papad — Laghu, crunchy, aids digestion alongside the Guru rice
A small side of fresh Thengai Chutney (28) — Sheeta cooling contrast
Pickle (small quantity) — Deepana sour stimulates Agni for the heavier meal
pairings_avoid: Paal (Milk) or any milk-based item — Sour Sambar + Milk is classical Viruddha Ahara
Cold beverages alongside — suppresses Agni mid-meal
Another grain-based item in the same meal — doubles the Guru burden

condition_warnings.advice: The mushy texture increases glycemic response compared to firm rice. Reduce rice ratio, increase vegetable content in Sambar. Eat with a small fat source (ghee or coconut) to slow glucose absorption.
Toor Dal (pigeon pea) in Sambar has moderate purines. Limit portion. The vegetables and tamarind are beneficial.
Excellent therapeutic food — soft texture, spiced, warm, complete nutrition. Classical Ayurvedic Pathya (medicinal diet) for convalescence.

red_flags: Very oily layer floating on top (Dalda or excess refined oil)
Uniform 'packet masala' Sambar taste — no aromatic depth
Firm, separate grains (leftover rice, not cooked together)
Very sour or very sweet Sambar (imbalanced)
No visible vegetables in the Sambar component

foodResults.name: Dosa
foodResults.dosha_summary: Pitta increasing when sour; Vata-Kapha beneficial when fresh
foodResults.agni_impact: Deepana when batter is fresh (active fermentation metabolites). Ama-forming when batter is old (excess acid, degraded proteins).
foodResults.digestibility: easy when fresh; moderate when sour
foodResults.best_meal_time: Breakfast — ideally before 8 AM when Agni is building
foodResults.question_asked: Is the batter freshly fermented (1–2 days maximum) and cooked on a well-seasoned iron or cast-iron tawa — not a non-stick coated pan?
foodResults.reasoning: Old batter (3+ days) develops excess lactic and acetic acid, degraded protein, and begins producing ethanol. This extreme Amla quality directly aggravates Pitta — Charaka lists excessive sour taste as a direct cause of Rakta dushana (blood vitiation), skin eruptions, and inflammation. Non-stick (Teflon) pans at high heat release PTFE fumes and have no iron transfer benefit.
ideal_seasons: Hemanta — the Ushna quality is welcome in cold seasons
avoid_seasons: Sharad (Autumn) — Pitta is already at peak; sour fermented food escalates it
foodResults.ritucharya_reason: Dosa's Amla vipaka naturally increases Pitta. In Pitta-dominant seasons (Grishma, Sharad), daily dosa creates cumulative Pitta aggravation. Once or twice per week is reasonable year-round; daily consumption in summer is inadvisable for Pitta types.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_vata: Good — the warm, thin, slightly sour crepe with oil is grounding. Prefer Set Dosa (thicker, softer) over Paper Roast.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_pitta: Moderate — fresh batter, no over-fermentation. Pair with cooling Thengai Chutney. Avoid red chutneys. Limit to once daily.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_kapha: Excellent — Laghu, Ruksha (dry) nature of crispy dosa is good for Kapha. Can eat Paper Roast version.
pairings_ideal: Thengai Chutney (28) — cooling Sheeta offsets Dosa's Ushna
pairings_avoid: Paal Kolukattai (17) — sour fermented + milk = Viruddha (Ama formation)
Cold milk or cold drinks immediately after — shock to Agni

condition_warnings.advice: Fresh batter only, soft dosa, with cooling chutneys. Over-sour batter is an immediate trigger.
Limit to once per week. The Amla vipaka accumulates Pitta in Rakta dhatu.
Rice-lentil batter is goitrogen-free. This is fine. However, avoid pairing with raw cruciferous vegetables.

foodResults.name: Kaara Chutney
foodResults.classification: Spiced Red Chili Chutney — Pitta Aggravating
foodResults.dosha_summary: Pitta strongly aggravating; Kapha pacifying (clears mucus); Vata aggravating in excess
foodResults.agni_impact: Initially strongly Deepana — stimulates Agni rapidly. However excess or daily use leads to Tivra Agni (hyperactive Agni) which damages the gut lining — Pitta disorder.
foodResults.ama_risk: low (itself Ama-clearing) but creates Pitta disorders with excess use
foodResults.best_meal_time: Small quantity at breakfast or lunch only
foodResults.question_asked: Did you temper it with Gingelly (sesame) oil and add a small amount of tamarind to balance the heat with a light sour note?
foodResults.positive_label: Yes — Gingelly Oil Tadka + Tamarind Balance
foodResults.answer_given: Yes — Gingelly Oil Tadka + Tamarind Balance
foodResults.reasoning: Gingelly (sesame) oil is Sheeta in long-term effect despite being Ushna in immediate action — it specifically counters the long-term Pitta aggravation of red chili. Charaka recommends sesame oil as the best oil for Pitta-Vata pacification. The tiny tamarind adds Amla rasa which acts as Anupana (vehicle) that distributes and moderates the chili's Teekshna quality across the gut without concentrating it.
ideal_seasons: Varsha (Monsoon — when Agni is weak and needs stimulation)
avoid_seasons: Sharad (Autumn) — Pitta seasons; this is adding fuel to fire literally
foodResults.ritucharya_reason: Kaara Chutney's job is to kindle Agni — in winter and monsoon this is valuable. In summer and autumn, Pitta is already elevated and needs cooling food, not Teekshna stimulation.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_vata: Use sparingly — very small amount with ghee-rich food. The Teekshna-Ushna quality dries Vata further. One tablespoon maximum.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_pitta: Strongly minimize. If Pitta is already imbalanced (heartburn, skin issues, anger), eliminate entirely. If Pitta is in balance, a tiny amount 2–3 times per week is acceptable.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_kapha: Beneficial in appropriate amounts — clears Kapha from channels, stimulates Agni, reduces mucus. Kapha can tolerate this best of all three types.
pairings_ideal: Idly (7) — 1 tablespoon max; the mild Idly absorbs and moderates the chutney's intensity
Medu Vadai (12) — small amount to counter Kapha from fried food
pairings_avoid: Any milk or dairy in same meal — Teekshna + Sheeta-Guru = direct Viruddha
Appam (0) — defeats the Pitta-pacifying benefit of Appam entirely

condition_warnings.advice: Absolute contraindication — even a small amount will trigger immediate aggravation.
Avoid entirely. Teekshna quality directly aggravates Rakta-Pitta through skin channels.
Contraindicated — this is Teekshna attacking already inflamed tissue.

foodResults.name: Mutton Biryani
foodResults.classification: Rice with Mutton — Heavy Rajasic Feast Food
foodResults.dosha_summary: Kapha-Pitta aggravating; beneficial for Vata when prepared correctly with digestive spices
foodResults.vipaka: Guru (Heavy — Madhura with heavy post-digestion load)
foodResults.agni_impact: Requires maximum Agni (Tikshna Agni) to digest without Ama. The whole spices are Deepana but the heavy fat and protein dominate post-digestively.

foodResults.positive_label: Good Quality Mutton + Full Digestive Spice Base + Ghee
foodResults.negative_label: Factory-Farmed Tough Mutton + Masala Powder Only + Cheap Oil
foodResults.answer_given: Good Quality Mutton + Full Digestive Spice Base + Ghee
foodResults.reasoning: Grass-fed/free-range mutton has 3-4x higher omega-3 fatty acids and lower inflammatory arachidonic acid vs factory-farmed. Ginger + pepper = Mamsala combination specifically recommended by Charaka for heavy meat preparations (increases protease enzyme activity for protein digestion). Fennel + mint = Vata-pacifying aromatic spices that moderate the Ushna virya of meat. Ghee specifically reduces meat's Ushna nature (per Charaka Samhita: Go-Ghrita + Mamsa = Pitta moderation) while building Ojas.
ideal_seasons: Hemanta (Nov–Dec) — Agni is at maximum strength
avoid_seasons: Sharad (Sep–Oct) — Pitta is elevated; adding Ushna-Guru mutton creates inflammatory cascade
foodResults.ritucharya_reason: Mutton is the heaviest meat in Ayurvedic classification. Only the body's winter-maximum Agni can process it without accumulating Ama in the Mamsa and Meda dhatus.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_vata: Best meat for Vata — heavy, warm, nourishing, and specifically Brimhana for Vata's depleted Dhatus. With proper digestive spices, this is Vata's feast food.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_pitta: Limit to once a month maximum. Ushna virya of mutton directly aggravates Pitta. Never eat with hot chutneys.
foodResults.prakriti_advice_kapha: Minimize or avoid — the heaviest food for the heaviest dosha. If eating, small portion with maximum pepper and no dessert after.
pairings_ideal: Plain Raita — probiotics for heavy protein
Fresh lime with salt — digestive Agni support
pairings_avoid: Paal Kolukattai (17) — Meat + Milk: critical Viruddha Ahara (skin disorders, allergic cascade)
Any cold drinks — suppresses Agni needed to process meat
Medu Vadai (12) or any other fried food in same meal

condition_warnings.advice: High purine content. Contraindicated during flare-ups. Strict limitation even in remission.
Avoid restaurant versions. Home version with good quality meat and ghee, once a month, may be acceptable under Vaidya guidance.
Contraindicated — Ushna virya of mutton + Pitta disorders = inflammatory aggravation.
